text: The surgeon introduces a Veress needle over the inferior crease of the umbilicus for laparoscopic access. The irrigation test, suction test, and hanging drop test are performed to confirm needle placement. A supraumbilical port is utilized for the procedure.

The surgeon introduces a 10mm trocar five centimeters above the umbilicus as an optical port for the telescope. Upon inserting the telescope, a diagnostic laparoscopy is performed, revealing the uterus covered by the omentum and multiple fibroids within the uterus.

The surgeon uses a myoma screw to hold the sub-serous fibroid in place. An incision is made with a harmonic scalpel, carefully cutting close to the fibroid to avoid damaging the uterine tissue. The fibroid is gradually excised from the uterus.

The surgeon collects removed fibroids in the right iliac fossa under direct visualization to prevent them from disappearing. A myoma screw is applied to another fibroid, with slight oozing observed, which will be addressed after removal. The fibroid is separated and deposited near the appendix. The third fibroid is located on the posterior uterine wall.

The surgeon separates the fourth fibroid located on the anterior wall, slightly to the right. This intramural fibroid is carefully enucleated and gradually separated from the surrounding uterine tissue.

The surgeon begins suturing using a number one Vicryl suture with a three-centimeter curved, round-bodied needle. A surgeon's knot is applied with a double wrap followed by two alternating wraps to secure the repair. The suturing process is crucial to ensure proper uterine repair, even though the fibroid was not deep.

The surgeon performs continuous suturing with interlocking stitches to secure the fibroid removal site, ensuring the sutures are tight. The final termination of the upper fibroid is completed, followed by suturing of the posterior fibroid. The surgeon applies a surgeon's knot and continues interlocking stitches, returning back a few times to reinforce the sutures using the starter tail.
The surgeon performs double-layer suturing on the uterine wall, with all stitches interlocked, and proceeds to terminate the suture line.

The surgical field shows no bleeding as the needle is removed. Suction irrigation is performed to clean the area, followed by morcellation to remove all four fibroids. The morcellation is carefully executed to avoid injury to surrounding structures, with visible peristalsis of the ureter below. The pelvis is thoroughly washed with normal saline, and the fluid is suctioned out.
The surgeon prepares to close the 15 mm morcellator port following the completion of the laparoscopic myomectomy procedure.
